32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
87	
88	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
89	
90	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
91	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
92	
93	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
94	
95	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait for it, open the new `references/<slug>.md`, then write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
96	
97	## What you must / must not do
98	
99	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
100	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
101	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
102	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
103	
104	## Workflow
105	
106	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
107	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
108	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
109	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
110	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
111	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
112	7. Write your report.
113

137	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
138	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
139	
140	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
141	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
142	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
143	
144	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
145	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
146

108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.b sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
8	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) -- a projective \(S\)-scheme representing the
9	% functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) with
10	% Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) on every fiber. The Quot construction is the
11	% \emph{existence engine} behind the relative Picard functor: A.2.c
12	% (\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) wires this chapter together with
13	% the relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, A.1.c)
14	% through the Abel map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), which factors
15	% through the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) -- the
16	% case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor. This chapter is gated on
17	% A.2.a (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}, written this iter) and
18	% A.1.a (\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}, on disk).
19	%
20	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The construction embeds Quot as a
21	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
22	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
23	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
24	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
25	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
26	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
27	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability; it is a

29	% \texttt{Picard\_GrassmannianScheme.tex} (writer iter-175+) -- see "Notes
30	% for Plan Agent" in the writer report.

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
52	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
53	% the verbatim source-quote in \ is taken from
54	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
55	% reference).
56	
57	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
58	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
59	
60	\begin{definition}\leanok
61	[Hilbert polynomial]
62	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
63	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
64	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
65	  % Polynomials" (read from
66	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
68	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
69	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
70	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
71	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
72	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
73	  %  the function
74	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
75	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
76	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
77	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
78	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
79	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
80	  %
81	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
82	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
83	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
84	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
85	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
86	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
87	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
88	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
89	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
90	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
91	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
92	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
93	  III.5.2.}
94	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
95	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
96	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), the
97	  \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\) relative to \(L\)} is the
98	  polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) characterised by
99	  \[
100	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
101	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
102	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}|_{X_s} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
103	  \]
104	  (a polynomial in \(m\) by Snapper's Lemma; the sum is finite by coherence and
105	  properness). When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
106	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
107	  well-defined on connected components.
108	\end{definition}
109	
110	\section{The Quot functor}
111	\label{sec:quot_functor}
112	
113	\begin{definition}\leanok
114	[Quot functor]
115	  \label{def:quot_functor}
116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
117	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial}
118	
119	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
120	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
121	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
122	  % + L481-L498).
123	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
124	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
125	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
126	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
127	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
128	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
129	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
130	  %
131	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
132	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
133	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
134	  %
135	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
136	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
137	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
138	  %
139	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
140	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
141	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
142	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
143	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
144	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
145	  %  well-defined, which gives
146	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
147	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
148	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
149	  %  [...]
150	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
151	  %  as a co-product
152	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
153	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
154	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
155	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
156	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
157	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
158	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
159	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
160	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
161	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
162	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
163	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
164	  \[
165	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
166	  \]
167	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
168	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
169	  \begin{enumerate}
170	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
171	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) and which is flat over \(T\);
172	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
173	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
174	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
175	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
176	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
177	      \(\Phi\).
178	  \end{enumerate}
179	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
180	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
181	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
182	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
183	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
184	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
185	\end{definition}
186	
187	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
188	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
189	
190	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
191	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
192	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
193	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
194	
195	\begin{definition}\leanok
196	[Grassmannian scheme]
197	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
199	  \uses{def:quot_functor}
200	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
201	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
202	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
203	  % + L773-L850).
204	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
205	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
206	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
207	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
208	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
209	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
210	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
211	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
212	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
213	  %  contravariant functor
214	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
215	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
216	  %  set of all equivalence classes
217	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
218	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
219	  %  [...]
220	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
221	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
222	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
223	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
224	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
225	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
226	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
227	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
228	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
229	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
230	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
231	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
232	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
233	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
234	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
235	  \[
236	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
237	    \qquad
238	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
239	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
240	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
241	  \]
242	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Concretely $\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) =
243	  \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\( (the Quot functor for \)X = S\(, \)E = V$,
244	  constant Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\)).
245	\end{definition}
246	
247	\begin{theorem}\leanok
248	[Representability of the Grassmannian]
249	  \label{thm:grassmannian_representable}
250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian.representable}
251	  \uses{def:grassmannian_scheme}
252	
253	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
254	  % affine patches" through "Projective embedding"
255	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
256	  % L798-L930).
257	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
258	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme
259	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological

289	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
290	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
291	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
292	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
293	  equipped with a tautological quotient
294	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
295	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
296	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
297	  $\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
298	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)$ -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
299	\end{theorem}
300	
301	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
302	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
303	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
304	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
305	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
306	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
307	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
308	\begin{proof}
309	  
310	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
311	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
312	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
313	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
314	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
315	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
316	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
317	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
318	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
319	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
320	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
321	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
322	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
323	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
324	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
325	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
326	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
327	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
328	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
329	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
330	
331	  The universal quotient $u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
332	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\( is given on \)U^I\( by the matrix \)X^I$
333	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
334	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
335	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
336	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
337	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
338	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
339	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
340	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
341	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
342	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
343	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
344	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
345	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism $T \to U^I \subset
346	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\(. Base change from \)\mathbb{Z}$ to a general noetherian
347	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
348	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
349	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
350	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
351	\end{proof}

394	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: this proof is long (~5 named sub-sections in [Nitsure]
395	% \S 5). Each sub-step is recorded with its own SOURCE QUOTE in the
396	% sub-lemmas below. The structural skeleton of the proof is restated in
397	% project notation in the \begin{proof} block.
398	\begin{proof}
399	  
400	  The proof has four steps; each step is a sub-lemma in the next
401	  sub-sections.
402	  \begin{enumerate}
403	    \item \textbf{(Boundedness, \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}.)} Pick the
404	      Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound $m = m(\mathrm{rank}\,V,
405	      \mathrm{rank}\,W, \Phi)$ uniformly across all fibers (by Mumford's
406	      \(m\)-regularity theorem applied to \(E_s\) and to all coherent
407	      quotients of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\)). For \(r \ge m\)
408	      and any \(T \to S\) and any \(T\)-flat quotient
409	      \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
410	      \(\Phi\), the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\)
411	      and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\) and \(R^i (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\)
412	      vanish for \(i \ge 1\) (where \(\mathcal{G} = \ker q\)), and the
413	      direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
414	      \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of ranks fixed by the
415	      data; the canonical maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\)
416	      are surjective. Cohomology-and-base-change (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology},
417	      Stacks tag 02KH) propagates these properties across \(T\)-base change.
418	    \item \textbf{(Grassmannian embedding, \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}.)}
419	      Reduce by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} to the case
420	      \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V, W\) locally free
421	      on \(S\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\). Fix \(r \ge m\).
422	      The Grassmannian embedding
423	      \[
424	        \alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
425	        \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
426	          W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
427	        \right)
428	      \]
429	      sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) to the locally free
430	      quotient \((\pi_T)_*(q(r)) : (\pi_T)_* E_T(r) \to (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\).
431	      This is injective on \(T\)-valued points: from the map $T \to
432	      \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))$ one recovers
433	      the kernel-pull-back \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r) \to E_T(r)\),
434	      whose cokernel is \(q(r) : E_T(r) \to \mathcal{F}(r)\);
435	      twisting back by \(-r\) recovers \(q\).
436	    \item \textbf{(Locally closed in the Grassmannian via flattening
437	      stratification.)} Let $T_0 = \mathrm{Gr}_S(W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V,
438	      \Phi(r))$ with universal quotient
439	      \(f : (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_{T_0} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{J}\)
440	      and kernel \(\mathcal{K}\). Form the composite
441	      $h : \pi_{T_0}^* \mathcal{K} \hookrightarrow
442	      \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V) = \pi_{T_0}^* (\pi_{T_0})_* E_{T_0}
443	      \twoheadrightarrow E_{T_0}$
444	      and let \(q := \mathrm{coker}(h) : E_{T_0} \to \mathcal{F}\). By the
445	      flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
446	      of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\)
447	      on \(X_{T_0}\), there exists a locally closed subscheme
448	      \(T_0^\Phi \subseteq T_0\) such that for any \(\phi : Y \to T_0\), the
449	      pulled-back cokernel \(\mathcal{F}_Y\) is \(Y\)-flat with fiberwise
450	      Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) iff \(\phi\) factors through \(T_0^\Phi\).
451	      The pair \((T_0^\Phi, \text{universal cokernel})\) represents
452	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\).
453	    \item \textbf{(Closed, hence projective,
454	      \cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}.)} The functor
455	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion of
456	      properness with respect to DVRs (Exercise (7) of \S 1, proved
457	      using that flatness over a DVR is torsion-freeness). Combined
458	      with the locally closed embedding of step 3 into the projective
459	      $\mathrm{Gr}_S \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^{\Phi(r)}
460	      (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V))$, this upgrades to a \emph{closed}
461	      embedding. Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a projective \(S\)-scheme.
462	  \end{enumerate}
463	  Finally, by \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}, the general case
464	  (arbitrary \(X\) projective over \(S\) and arbitrary coherent \(E\)) follows
465	  from the case \(X = \mathbb{P}(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\): pick a closed
466	  embedding \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) and write \(E\) as a coherent quotient
467	  of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)\) for some vector bundle \(W\) on \(S\) and integer \(\nu\);
468	  the Quot scheme of the original data is then a closed subscheme of the
469	  Quot scheme of the universal data, by the closed-embedding clause of
470	  \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}.
471	\end{proof}

610	  of rank \(p\), so \(E_s\) is \(0\)-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
611	  \(m\)-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
612	  polynomial \(\Phi\), a uniform integer \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\) above which
613	  every coherent quotient of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) -- and
614	  its kernel -- is \(m\)-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
615	  converts \(m\)-regularity into vanishing of \(H^i((-) (r))\) for \(i \ge 1\)
616	  and \(r \ge m\), and into global generation of \(H^0((-)(r))\). To
617	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over \(T\), apply the

620	  base change, so the direct images \((\pi_T)_*(-)(r)\) are locally free of
621	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
622	  maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\) inherit fiberwise
623	  surjectivity.

718	  map

720	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)

730	  let \(j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R\) be the open inclusion. Define
731	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) as the image of the composite
732	  \(E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K\), with induced surjection
733	  \(\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}\). The sheaf
734	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) is torsion-free over \(R\) (being a subsheaf of
735	  \(j_* \mathcal{F}_K\)), hence \(R\)-flat (flatness over a DVR

740	  open \(X_K \subset X_R\) and is torsion-free, hence equals the image

742	  \(\Spec R\) by flatness.

20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}

86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\begin{theorem}
98	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
99	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
100	  
101	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
102	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
105	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
106	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
107	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
109	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
110	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
111	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
112	\end{theorem}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
115	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
116	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
117	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
118	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
119	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
120	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
121	% there exists a finite filtration
122	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
123	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
124	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
125	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
126	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
127	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
128	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
129	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
130	\begin{proof}
131	  
132	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
133	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
134	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
135	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
136	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
137	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
138	  finite \(B\)-module
139	  \[
140	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
141	  \]
142	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
143	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
144	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
145	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
146	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
147	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
148	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
149	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
150	  \[
151	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
152	  \]
153	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
154	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
155	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
156	\end{proof}

185	% Theorem~REF by restricting to a non-empty
186	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
187	\begin{proof}
188	  
189	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
190	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
191	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
192	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). Theorem~REF
193	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
194	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
195	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
196	\end{proof}

279	\begin{proof}
280	  \uses{def:coherent_sheaf_flat, lem:flat_locus_open, lem:nonflat_locus_proper,
281	    lem:noetherian_induction_strata, thm:generic_flatness}
282	  Following [Nitsure], \S4, the construction reduces to the special case
283	  \(n = 0\), assembled over the relative twists \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\).  As the
284	  argument is local on \(S\) (the strata glue by their universal property, part
285	  (ii)), and as the three conclusions are proved through the sub-lemmas below,
286	  we assemble them in the order (i), (ii), (iii).
287	
288	  \emph{Part (i): finiteness of the Hilbert-polynomial set.}
289	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}, Noetherian induction on \(S\) (whose
290	  inductive step invokes generic flatness, Theorem~\ref{thm:generic_flatness},
291	  on the integral locally-closed strata cut out by the irreducible-component
292	  decomposition of \(S\)) produces finitely many reduced, pairwise disjoint,
293	  locally-closed subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\) set-theoretically covering
294	  \(|S|\) on each of which \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) in
295	  the sense of Definition~\ref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  On a flat family the
296	  Hilbert polynomial \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is locally constant, so
297	  it takes finitely many values on each quasi-compact \(V_i\); the finite union
298	  over the finitely many \(V_i\) shows that the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
299	  occurring on \(S\) is finite.
300	
301	  \emph{Part (ii): existence of the strata and their universal property.}
302	  Working over the flat pieces \(V_i\) of part (i), semicontinuity
303	  (cohomology-and-base-change) yields a uniform integer \(N\) such that, for all
304	  \(s \in S\) and \(m \ge N\), the higher cohomologies \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\)
305	  vanish for \(r \ge 1\) and the base-change homomorphism
306	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism.  With this
307	  \(N\) fixed, Lemma~\ref{lem:noetherian_induction_strata} applies the \(n = 0\)
308	  stratification mechanism (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}) successively to the
309	  coherent sheaves \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\),
310	  refining stratum by stratum.  After \(n+1\) steps this produces a finite
311	  family of locally-closed subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\),
312	  universal for the property that each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free sheaf
313	  of constant rank \(e_i\); re-indexing the tuple \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
314	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\)
315	  gives the family \(\{W_f\}_{f \in I}\).  At any \(s \in W_f\) the base-change
316	  isomorphism and the cohomology vanishing force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has
317	  degree \(\le n\) and is determined by its values at \(N, \dots, N+n\)), so
318	  \(|W_f| = \{s : P_s = f\}\); these subsets are disjoint with union \(|S|\).
319	  The required locally-closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) is the closed
320	  subscheme cut out by the further local-freeness conditions
321	  ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally free of rank \(f(N+i)\) for all
322	  \(i \ge 0\)'' (a chain of coherent ideal sheaves that stabilises by
323	  Noetherianity), and \(\F|_{\PP^n_{S_f}}\) is flat over \(S_f\)
324	  (Definition~\ref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}) because the graded module
325	  \(\bigoplus_{i \gg 0} \pi_* \F(i)|_{S_f}\) is locally free.  Forming
326	  \(S' = \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the assembled morphism \(i : S' \to S\),
327	  the pullback \(i^* \F\) is \(S'\)-flat; and any \(\varphi : T \to S\) with
328	  \(\varphi^* \F\) flat over \(T\) has locally constant Hilbert polynomial, hence
329	  decomposes \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed loci where the polynomial
330	  is \(f\), each factoring uniquely through the corresponding \(S_f\) by the
331	  local-freeness universal property of Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}.  This
332	  gives the unique factorisation through \(i\), and shows each \(S_f\) is
333	  uniquely determined by \(f\).
334	
335	  \emph{Part (iii): closure of strata.}
336	  Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\).  Since each
337	  \(S_f\) is a stratum of the \(n = 0\) flattening stratification applied to a
338	  single direct image \(\pi_* \F(p)\) for \(p \gg 0\) separating all polynomials
339	  in \(I\), the closure-of-strata assertion reduces to the upper-semicontinuity
340	  statement already established in the \(n = 0\) case
341	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}): the closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is
342	  contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
343	\end{proof}

531	  Theorem~REF.  There exists an integer
532	  \(N \ge 0\) such that for every \(s \in S\) the higher cohomologies
533	  \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) vanish for all \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N\), and the base-change
534	  homomorphism \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism
535	  for \(m \ge N\), \(s \in S\).  Iterating the \(n=0\) stratification
536	  (Lemma~REF) on the coherent sheaves \(E_i := \pi_* \F(N+i)\)
537	  for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) produces a finite collection of locally-closed
538	  subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\) indexed by tuples
539	  \((e_0, \dots, e_n) \in \Z^{n+1}\). Re-indexing each tuple by the unique
540	  numerical polynomial \(f \in \Q[\lambda]\) of degree \(\le n\) with
541	  \(f(N+i) = e_i\) gives a finite stratification \(\{W_f\}\) of \(S\), and the
542	  locally-closed subschemes \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) of
543	  Theorem~REF are obtained by further
544	  intersecting with the closed-subscheme conditions that
545	  \(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) be locally free of rank \(f(N+i)\) for all \(i \ge 0\)
546	  (terminating by Noetherianity).
547	\end{lemma}
548	
549	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: full proof is the long body of [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem
550	% ``flattening stratification'', spanning L1844-L2090 (~250 lines: special
551	% case, Noetherian induction, statements (A)/(B)/(C), then the assembly via
552	% direct images and the closed-subscheme construction inside each \(W_f\)).
553	% Verbatim retrieved per-sub-lemma above; the structural restatement below in
554	% project notation is the canonical Nitsure-style restated proof.
555	\begin{proof}
556	  
557	  By Lemma~REF, the family of Hilbert polynomials
558	  \(\{P_s\}\) on \(S\) is finite, and there is a finite stratification
559	  \(\{V_i\}\) of \(S\) on each of which \(\F\) is flat with locally-constant
560	  Hilbert polynomial.  Apply semi-continuity (cohomology and base change,
561	  [Hartshorne], III.12 / [Stacks Project], tag 02KH for the \(H^0\)-version) on
562	  the noetherian \(V_i\) to obtain a uniform integer \(N_1\) with
563	  \(R^r \pi_* \F(m) = 0\) for \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\), and \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m)) = 0\)
564	  for all \(s \in S\), \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\) (statement (B) of Nitsure's proof).
565	  Choose \(r_i \ge N_1\) on each \(V_i\) such that the base-change comparison
566	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_{V_i} \to (\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m)\) is an isomorphism for
567	  \(m \ge r_i\); composing with the cohomology-and-base-change isomorphism
568	  \(((\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m))|_s \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) for
569	  the flat family on \(V_i\) yields uniform \(N = \max r_i\) such that
570	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism for \(m \ge N\)
571	  and all \(s \in S\) (statement (C)).
572	
573	  Apply Lemma~REF sequentially to the coherent sheaves
574	  \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\): \(E_0\) produces a
575	  stratification \(\{W_{e_0}\}\) of \(S\); on each \(W_{e_0}\) apply the lemma to
576	  \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\) to refine to \(\{W_{e_0, e_1}\}\), and continue. After \(n+1\)
577	  steps the \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n}\) form a finite locally-closed stratification
578	  of \(S\) universal for the property ``each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free
579	  \(\OO_T\)-module of constant rank \(e_i\)''.  Re-index \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
580	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\) to get
581	  the family \(\{W_f\}\) indexed by polynomials.  At any \(s \in W_f\), statement
582	  (C) and vanishing (B) force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has degree \(\le n\) and is
583	  determined by its values at \(N, N+1, \dots, N+n\)).
584	
585	  The Hilbert-polynomial-coincidence locus \(|W_f|\) may have non-flat scheme
586	  structure; refine to \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) by intersecting with the
587	  closed-subscheme conditions ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally-free of rank
588	  \(f(N+i)\)'' for all \(i \ge 0\).  These conditions are cut out by an increasing
589	  chain of coherent ideal sheaves \(I_0 \subseteq I_0 + I_1 \subseteq \cdots\) on
590	  \(W_f\); Noetherianity terminates the chain in finitely many steps, defining a
591	  coherent ideal sheaf \(I\) and a closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) with
592	  \(|S_f| = |W_f|\) on which \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) is locally-free of rank \(f(N+i)\)
593	  for all \(i \ge 0\). The base-change-without-flatness lemma (cf.\ [Nitsure],
594	  \S3) yields some \(N' \ge N\) such that for \(i \ge N'\),
595	  \((\pi_* \F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism, hence
596	  the graded \(A\)-module \(\bigoplus_{i \ge N'} (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is
597	  locally-free on \(S_f\), hence \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\). The
598	  closure-of-strata property (iii) reduces to the corresponding property in
599	  the \(n=0\) case for \(\pi_* \F(p)\) on \(S\) at any \(p \ge N\) separating all
600	  Hilbert polynomials.
601	\end{proof}
602	
603	\section{Universal property of the strata}
604	\label{sec:flatstrat_universal}
605	
606	\begin{theorem}
607	
608	\leanok
609	[Universal property of the flattening stratification]
610	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_universal}
611	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal}
612	  \uses{thm:flattening_stratification_exists, lem:noetherian_induction_strata}
613	
614	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', part (ii)
615	  % universal property
616	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1826-L1834).
617	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
618	  % "\textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
619	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
620	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
621	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
622	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
623	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
624	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
625	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
626	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\)."
627	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (part (ii) of the main theorem); cf.\ [Stacks
628	  Project], tag 052H.}
629	  Let \(S\), \(\F\), and \(\{S_f\}_{f \in I}\) be as in
630	  Theorem~REF.  Set
631	  \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) with the coproduct morphism
632	  \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the locally-closed inclusions.  Then \(i^* \F\)
633	  is flat over \(S'\), and the pair \((S', i)\) is universal: for any morphism
634	  \(\varphi : T \to S\) of noetherian schemes (one may take \(T\) arbitrary, the
635	  Noetherian hypothesis on \(S\) being preserved), the pullback
636	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is \(T\)-flat if and only if \(\varphi\) factors
637	  uniquely through \(i\). In particular each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
638	\end{theorem}
639	
640	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that the morphism \(\coprod_f S_f \,\to \,S\)
641	% indeed has the property (ii) of the theorem.
642	% By Lemma REF,
643	% there exists some \(N' \ge N\) such that for all \(i\ge N'\),
644	% the base-change
645	% \((\pi_*\F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_*\F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism for
646	% each \(S_f\). Therefore
647	% \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\) by Lemma REF,
648	% as the direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N'\) are locally
649	% free over \(S_f\). Conversely, if \(\phi : T\to S\) is a morphism such
650	% that \(\F_T\) is flat, then the Hilbert polynomial is locally
651	% constant over \(T\). Let \(T_f\) be the open and closed subscheme of \(T\)
652	% where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\). Clearly, the set
653	% map \(|T_f| \to |S|\) factors via \(|S_f|\). But as the direct images
654	% \({\pi_T}_*\F_T(i)\) are locally free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\), it
655	% follows in fact that the schematic morphism \(T_f \to S\) factors
656	% via \(S_f\), proving the property (ii) of the theorem."
657	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2065-L2082.)
658	\begin{proof}
659	  
660	  Flatness of \(i^* \F\) over \(S'\) was established at the end of the proof of
661	  Theorem~REF (the
662	  base-change-without-flatness lemma plus the graded-module-flatness criterion
663	  applied to the coherent sheaves \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) on each \(S_f\)).
664	  Conversely, if \(\varphi : T \to S\) satisfies \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is
665	  \(T\)-flat, then \(T\)-flatness of the family forces the Hilbert polynomial to be
666	  locally constant on \(T\).  Decompose \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed
667	  subschemes where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\).  The set map \(|T_f| \to |S|\)
668	  factors through \(|S_f|\); further, since the direct images
669	  \((\pi_T)_* \varphi^* \F(i)\) are locally-free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\) for $i
670	  \ge N'\(, the universal property of \)S_f \subseteq W_f$ as the closed
671	  subscheme cut out by these local-freeness conditions
672	  (Lemma~REF) yields a unique scheme
673	  morphism \(T_f \to S_f\) extending the set map. Assembling over \(f\) gives the
674	  unique factorisation \(\varphi = i \circ \tilde \varphi\).
675	\end{proof}
676	
677	\section{Application: relative curves}
678	\label{sec:flatstrat_curve_application}
679	
680	In the Route~A consumer setting, the input to the flattening-stratification
681	machinery is the pullback \(C \times_k T \to T\) where \(C\) is a smooth proper
682	curve over a field \(k\) and \(T\) is any \(k\)-scheme.  The hypotheses of
683	Nitsure's Theorem~REF demand
684	\emph{projective} relative dimension, not merely \emph{proper}; we record that
685	no extra work is required to instantiate the theorem in our setting.
686	
687	\begin{lemma}
688	[Smooth proper curve over \(k\) is projective]
689	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective}
690	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6 (curve \(\Rightarrow\) quasi-projective;
691	  % proper \(\Rightarrow\) projective for curves), and standard consequences in
692	  % chapter III. The precise statement ``every smooth proper curve over a field
693	  % is projective'' is one of the classical equivalences for \(\dim 1\) and is
694	  % textbook material; this block is project-bespoke (no verbatim source
695	  % quote is reproduced here as the result is folklore-level and cited only
696	  % structurally for the Route~A reduction).
697	  \textit{Source: classical; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.6.7 \& II.7.6 (curves).}
698	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\).  Then \(C \to \Spec k\) is
699	  projective.  Consequently, for any noetherian \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the relative
700	  curve \(C \times_k T \to T\) is projective in the sense of Grothendieck (there
701	  exists a relatively very ample line bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\)).
702	\end{lemma}
703	
704	\begin{proof}
705	  Projectivity of a smooth proper curve over a field is classical
706	  ([Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6; alternatively: every divisor of large
707	  positive degree on a smooth proper curve \(C\) is very ample, providing the
708	  embedding \(C \hookrightarrow \PP^N_k\) for some \(N\)). Pulling the ample line
709	  bundle back along \(C \times_k T \to C\) produces a relatively very ample line
710	  bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\).
711	\end{proof}
712	
713	\begin{corollary}
714	[Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative curve]
715	  \label{cor:flattening_stratification_curve}
716	  
717	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a smooth proper curve over \(k\), and \(T\) a noetherian
718	  \(k\)-scheme.  Let \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(C \times_k T\). Then the
719	  flattening-stratification conclusion of
720	  Theorem~REF applies: there is a finite
721	  set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials and locally-closed subschemes
722	  \(T_f \hookrightarrow T\) for \(f \in I\), set-theoretically covering \(T\)
723	  disjointly, such that (a) \(\F|_{C \times_k T_f}\) is flat over \(T_f\) for
724	  each \(f\), and (b) any morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) such that
725	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(C \times_k T'\) is \(T'\)-flat factors uniquely through some
726	  \(T_f\).
727	\end{corollary}
728	
729	\begin{proof}
730	  
731	  By Lemma~REF, \(C \times_k T \to T\) is
732	  projective, hence factors through a closed immersion into \(\PP^n_T\) for some
733	  \(n \ge 0\).  Push \(\F\) forward along the closed immersion (extending by zero
734	  outside the image) to a coherent sheaf \(\tilde \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\); this
735	  operation preserves \(T\)-flatness in both directions because the closed
736	  immersion is a finite morphism.  Apply
737	  Theorem~REF to \(\tilde \F\) on
738	  \(\PP^n_T\) to obtain \(\{T_f\}\) with the stated universal property.
739	\end{proof}
740	
741	\section{Mathlib status}
742	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
743	
744	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides:
745	\begin{itemize}
746	  \item \texttt{Module.Flat} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}) --- the
747	    module-theoretic notion of flatness, applicable affine-locally to the
748	    stalks \(\F_x\).
749	  \item \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Flat} ---
750	    \texttt{IsFlat} as a morphism property and basic stability lemmas; this is
751	    flatness of the structure morphism, \emph{not} flatness of a quasi-coherent
752	    module over the base.
753	  \item \texttt{Module.FaithfullyFlat} and the descent infrastructure that
754	    consumes it.
755	\end{itemize}
756	
757	\noindent What Mathlib does \emph{not} yet have, and that this chapter will
758	introduce in the new file
759	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}:
760	\begin{itemize}
761	  \item A predicate \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
762	    capturing \(S\)-flatness of a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module on a finite-type
763	    \(S\)-scheme (Definition~REF). This is the
764	    sheaf-level lift of \texttt{Module.Flat} via the affine-local
765	    characterization in the second paragraph of the definition.
766	  \item The constructive existence proof
767	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
768	    (Theorem~REF) verbatim from
769	    Nitsure~\S4.  The proof decomposes into the three sub-lemmas
770	    \texttt{flatLocusStratification} (Lemma~REF),
771	    \texttt{flatLocusReduction} (Lemma~REF, the
772	    Noetherian-induction reduction via generic flatness), and
773	    \texttt{flatLocusAssembly} (Lemma~REF).
774	  \item The universal-property packaging
775	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification\_universal}
776	    (Theorem~REF) as a
777	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy}-style universal arrow.
778	  \item The relative-curve specialisation
779	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification.ofCurve}
780	    (Corollary~REF) for the Route~A
781	    consumer.
782	\end{itemize}
783	
784	Inputs from Mathlib that the proof consumes:
785	\begin{itemize}
786	  \item \texttt{CategoryTheory.Limits.Cofan} / \texttt{Sigma} for the coproduct
787	    \(\coprod_f S_f\).
788	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocallyClosedImmersion} (and the locally-closed
789	    subscheme API) for the strata \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\).
790	  \item \texttt{IsNoetherian} / \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Noetherian} for the
791	    Noetherian-induction termination argument.
792	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProjective} (or
793	    \texttt{ProjectiveMorphism}) and the relative-\(\PP^n\) construction, to
794	    state and unpack the projective-morphism hypothesis on
795	    \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\).
796	  \item Higher-direct-image base-change lemmas (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry
797	    .Cohomology}, or a Stacks-tag-02KH-shaped lemma; cf.\ the existing
798	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} use of the same tag) for the \(E_i = \pi_*\F(N+i)\)
799	    construction in Lemma~REF.
800	  \item Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity / Serre vanishing for the
801	    \texttt{N\_1}-existence step.  Mathlib does not yet have these in the
802	    relative form needed; a future sub-lemma may stub them or rely on
803	    \texttt{Module.IsTorsion} arguments on the graded module
804	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge N} \pi_* \F(m)\).
805	\end{itemize}
806	
807	\section{Out of scope}
808	\label{sec:flatstrat_out_of_scope}
809	
810	This chapter packages \emph{only} the flattening stratification of a coherent
811	sheaf on a projective morphism, plus the relative-curve specialisation. The
812	following downstream / parallel constructions live in separate sibling
813	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
814	\begin{itemize}
815	  \item \textbf{Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity} (Nitsure~\S2) --- input to the
816	    Quot construction, used implicitly inside the proof of
817	    Lemma~REF as the assertion that the
818	    Hilbert function stabilises beyond a uniform \(N\).  Its own blueprint home
819	    is the planned chapter \texttt{Picard\_CastelnuovoMumford.tex} (not yet
820	    scaffolded).
821	  \item \textbf{Quot scheme construction} (Nitsure~\S5, A.2.b) --- consumer of
822	    the present chapter; its home is the planned \texttt{Picard\_QuotScheme.tex}.
823	  \item \textbf{Relative Picard functor} (A.1.c) ---
824	    \texttt{Picard\_RelPicFunctor.tex} (planned).
825	  \item \textbf{Picard scheme representability for a smooth proper curve}
826	    (A.2.c) --- the assembly theorem fed by both the Quot construction (which
827	    consumes this chapter) and the relative Picard functor; home is the
828	    planned \texttt{Picard\_FGAPicRepresentability.tex}.
829	\end{itemize}
830

theorem flatteningStratification.ofCurve {k : Type u} [Field k]
